title: An Air of Camelot on the Champs-Elysees
summary: Though hardly in the dimensions of Charles de Gaulle, the new French President resembles his predecessor of the far past in claiming to represent not a political party but a movement ("La Republique en Marche!"), embracing both the left and the right.
body: "He's young, he's handsome, he’s intelligent," exulted a French matron in the huge crowd along the Champs-Elysees as they watched the JFK-like Emmanuel Macron stride among them on Monday, May 15. Macron had just drubbed his populist, far-right rival, Marine Le Pen, 66 percent to 33 percent in the presidential vote. As the French do it in an unparalleled way, earning the bon mot that France is a monarchy disguised as a republic, the transition of power went off with pomp and precision. It was accompanied by handshakes and cheek-pats between the outgoing Socialist president, a slightly fattened Francois Hollande, and his former economics minister, now his successor. In a gesture of support for the military, Macron took the unprecedented step of riding down the Champs Elysees at the head of the parade in a military rather than a civilian vehicle and of visiting three wounded French soldiers in this first day of his tenure. He has named as his prime minister Edouard Philippe, a tall, center-right politician who likes to box in his spare time. Though hardly in the dimensions of Charles de Gaulle, the new French President resembles his predecessor of the far past in claiming to represent not a political party but a movement ("La Republique en Marche!"), embracing both the left and the right. Without a political party, the 39-year-old Macron, the youngest French president ever, faces a huge challenge in the upcoming parliamentary elections in June. Lacking a political base, he will have to depend on the momentum effect of a "French spring." Judging from the enthusiasm of the turnout last Monday, he may well snap he French out of their legendary morosity. result.main_content_en: The article describes the inauguration of Emmanuel Macron as President of France following his decisive victory over Marine Le Pen. It highlights the ceremonial transition of power, noting Macron's symbolic gestures of military support, including riding in a military vehicle and visiting wounded soldiers. Macron, leading the 'La République en Marche!' movement rather than a traditional party, appointed Édouard Philippe as Prime Minister. The piece emphasizes the challenge Macron faces in the upcoming parliamentary elections due to his lack of an established political base, relying instead on the momentum of a 'French spring' to overcome political fragmentation and public pessimism.
result.main_content_zh: 本文描述了埃马纽埃尔·马克龙在击败玛丽娜·勒庞后当选法国总统的就职场景。文章重点描述了权力交接的仪式感，包括马克龙乘坐军用车辆检阅以及慰问受伤士兵等象征性支持军队的举动。马克龙以“共和国前进党”运动而非传统政党的领袖身份执政，并任命爱德华·菲利普为总理。文章强调，由于缺乏稳固的政治基础，马克龙在即将到来的议会选举中面临巨大挑战，他需要依靠“法国之春”的势头效应来克服政治碎片化和公众的悲观情绪。
